title: Neutron and X-ray Diffraction Reveal the Limits of Long-Range Machine Learning Potentials for Medium-Range Order in Silica Glass
authors: Sai Harshit Balantrapu, Atul C. Thakur, Chris Benmore, Ganesh Sivaraman
abstract: Glassy silica is a foundational material in optics and electronics, yet accurately predicting its medium-range order (MRO) remains a major challenge for machine-learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs). While local MLIPs reproduce the short-range SiO4 tetrahedral network well, it remains unclear whether locality alone is sufficient to recover the first sharp diffraction peak (FSDP), the principal experimental signature of MRO. Here, we combine neutron and X-ray diffraction measurements with large-scale molecular dynamics driven by two MACE-based models: a short-range (SR) potential and a long-range (LR) extension incorporating reciprocal-space gated attention. The SR model systematically over-structures the network, producing an overly intense FSDP in both the liquid and glassy states. Incorporating long-range interactions improves agreement with experiment for the liquid structure by reducing this excess ordering, but the LR model still fails to recover the experimental amorphous MRO after quenching. Ring-statistics and bond-angle analyses reveal that SR model exhibits an artificially narrow distribution dominated by six-membered rings, while the LR model produces a broader but still biased ring population. Despite preserving the correct tetrahedral geometry, both models show limited variability in Si-O-Si angles, indicating constrained network flexibility. These structural signatures demonstrate that both models retain excessive memory of the parent liquid network, leading to kinetically trapped and nonphysical medium-range configurations during vitrification. These results show that explicit long-range interactions are necessary but not sufficient for predictive modelling of disordered silica and suggest that accurate MRO further requires training data and sampling strategies that adequately represent the liquid-to-glass transition.
summary: This paper investigates the limits of machine learning interatomic potentials in capturing the medium-range order of silica glass. By comparing short-range and long-range MACE models against experimental X-ray and neutron diffraction, the authors find that while long-range interactions improve the liquid structure, they still fail to accurately predict the glass structure. The results suggest that simply increasing the interaction range is insufficient; accurate modeling also requires better sampling of the liquid-to-glass transition to avoid kinetic trapping in nonphysical configurations.
main_problem: Determining whether extending the interaction range in machine-learning interatomic potentials is sufficient to accurately predict the medium-range order (MRO) in silica glass.
main_result: Long-range interactions are necessary but not sufficient for predictive modeling of silica MRO, as both models exhibit kinetic trapping and fail to fully recover experimental structural features after quenching.
method: The study combines neutron and X-ray diffraction measurements with large-scale molecular dynamics simulations using two MACE-based machine learning potentials (short-range and long-range).
model_or_system: The physical system is amorphous silica (SiO2). The models compared are a short-range MACE potential (5 A cutoff) and a long-range MACE extension incorporating Reciprocal-Space Gated Attention (RSGA).
key_observables: First Sharp Diffraction Peak (FSDP), structure factor S(Q), ring-size distributions, Si-O-Si and O-Si-O bond angles, and persistent homology features.
important_parameters: Medium-range order (5-20 A), liquid-to-glass transition, and quenching rates.
main_assumptions: The models are trained on DFT-derived energies and forces using the SCAN functional; the study assumes that the primary limitation lies in the sampling/training data rather than the architecture itself.
figures_summary: Figures show the initial simulation cell, parity plots for energy/force accuracy, X-ray/neutron structure factors, bond-angle distributions, ring-size statistics, and persistent homology diagrams.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of MRO in silica, describes the development of SR and LR MACE models, presents validation against DFT, compares simulated structural properties (angles, rings, S(Q)) against experimental diffraction data, and concludes with implications for future training strategies.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
